estimation_meta.method: Values read from paper figures (approximate, log-scale charts)

footnotes: XPIR (2016) is a system paper with auto-parameter optimization. Benchmarks span many DB sizes via an automated search over recursion depths and ring parameters — no single fixed configs to list. Values read from Figures 6–8 (log-scale).

estimation_meta.method: Analytical cost model from Section 5.2 and Appendix A: rate=4/9, ~1.72 mults/byte, ring dim 2^12, q~2^46. Query size from Appendix A online component.
estimation_meta.reference_db_note: MulPIR is designed for large entries (>=100 KB); 256B entries are below optimal regime

asymptotic.response_size: O(L * n'_1 * n'_2 * d * log q)
asymptotic.server_computation: Õ(log log λ + log log log N) per bit

estimation_meta.method: Values read from paper figures (approximate, log-scale Figure 9; numeric labels from Figure 10)

footnotes: Addra/FastPIR has two distinct evaluation contexts: PIR microbenchmarks (Table 1: 32B entries at various N) and the Addra voice-call system benchmark (Section 6.5: 32,768 mailboxes × 96B messages). reported.json uses the system benchmark (32768 × 96B). Values are Tier 2 (approximate from figures).

asymptotic.server_computation: O(n * k * M + n * s * PM)

footnotes: CwPIR's benchmark configs (2^20–2^28 × 288B) match SealPIR's configs because CwPIR is evaluated as a drop-in replacement for SealPIR. Entry size is BFV plaintext payload; CwPIR is keyword PIR — the "288B" is the record payload, not the identifier.
FrodoPIR tests a grid of 5 N values (2^16 to 2^20) × 2 entry sizes (1 KB, 8 KB) = 10 configurations total (only endpoints shown). All in Table 6.

estimation_meta.method: Concrete numbers from Table 2 (proven security variant) at N=2^30, 360-byte entries. Server time derived from E2E time / Q queries.
estimation_meta.reference_db_note: N=2^30 with 360-byte entries; different scale from standard Group A reference

footnotes: ThorPIR has concrete parameter choices in Table 2 (N=2^30, 360B entries, 1024-query budget) but no running implementation. Server time derived from end-to-end time / Q queries. The parameters are the paper's own specification, not our estimate — hence "Analytical" rather than "Estimated."

asymptotic.server_computation: O(N * n * log q / log t)

footnotes: HintlessPIR fixes N=2^20 and scales entry size (288B to 8KB to 2MB). The 2MB entry config produces ~560 GB total DB — the largest tested by any scheme — but with only 2^20 ≈ 1M entries.

asymptotic.query_size: O(sqrt(N) * log q / log p + d * log d * log q)

footnotes: YPIR is optimized for the 1-bit PIR setting. Configs reflect Table 2 (1/8/32 GB, 1-bit entries). The paper also evaluates competitor schemes at 256B entries for comparison, but those are not YPIR's own configs.
WhisPIR values are Tier 2 — read from log-scale figures, not exact tables. Paper also tests up to 32 GB in Figure 4, but specific (N, entry_size) pairs are not clearly separable from the charts.
SimplePIR and DoublePIR keep total DB size constant at ~1 GB and vary the entry size / entry count tradeoff. All four configs represent the same ~1 GB database accessed differently.

VeriSimplePIR evaluates a scaling benchmark from 4 GiB to 256 GiB. Figures 7–8 show performance as a function of DB size on log-scale axes. Individual (N, entry_size) pairs not extractable — the paper plots continuous scaling curves, not discrete configurations.

estimation_meta.method: Asymptotic formulas from Theorem 3.1 with N=2^33 bits (1 GB). Server O(N/log^2 N) gives ~900x improvement over O(N), but poly(λ) and Williams' technique constants are unknown and potentially large.
estimation_meta.reference_db_note: 1 GB reference; poly(λ) factors make concrete estimates highly uncertain

footnotes: BarelyDoublyEfficient is a pure theory/feasibility result. "1 GB" was chosen for asymptotic estimation in reported.json. The paper proves existence via Williams' technique but the poly(λ) constants are unknown and potentially large. No concrete parameters specified in the paper.

IncrementalPIR evaluates iSimplePIR across a wide range (2^10 to 2^30 entries, 1-bit to 32-bit entries). The primary benchmark is 1 GB with 1-bit entries (matching SimplePIR baseline). The paper also evaluates a 4 GB password-breach detection scenario.

estimation_meta.method: Theorem 14 (two-server computational) with s=sqrt(n), λ=128, n=2^28 bits. Online comm O(λ^2 * log n) = ~56 KB. Server time includes unknown poly(λ, log n) factor.
estimation_meta.reference_db_note: N=2^20 x 32B; poly(λ) factors make server time estimate highly uncertain

footnotes: CK20, IshaiShiWichs, Plinko, and WangRen have no implementations. "N=2^20 × 32B" was chosen by the notes author to match Group D conventions and produce comparable asymptotic estimates. The papers themselves only state bounds in terms of N, not specific benchmark configurations.

estimation_meta.method: Construction 2 (Theorem 4.4): server Õ(n^{2/3}), comm Õ(n^{1/3}) at n=2^28 bits. Information-theoretic scheme; Õ hides omega(log n) repetition factors.
estimation_meta.reference_db_note: N=2^20 x 32B; super-logarithmic hidden factors from omega(log n) parallel repetition

footnotes: CK20, IshaiShiWichs, Plinko, and WangRen have no implementations. "N=2^20 × 32B" was chosen by the notes author to match Group D conventions and produce comparable asymptotic estimates.

estimation_meta.method: Asymptotic Õ(n/r) scaling at r=sqrt(n) for N=2^20 entries x 32B. Query comm n/r entries; response is single entry. Server does n/r probes (XOR + iPRF). No implementation benchmarks exist.
estimation_meta.reference_db_note: N=2^20 x 32B; Õ hides polylog factors from iPRF inversion

footnotes: CK20, IshaiShiWichs, Plinko, and WangRen have no implementations. "N=2^20 × 32B" was chosen by the notes author to match Group D conventions and produce comparable asymptotic estimates.

estimation_meta.method: Theorem 4.1 balanced tradeoff with T=sqrt(n)=sqrt(2^20)=1024 at N=2^20 entries x 32B. Paper uses n=number of entries (not bits). Query = T indices of log(n)=20 bits each = 2.5 KB. Response = T entries of w=32B each = 32 KB. Server does T=1024 memory reads (~0.1 ms, calibrated vs RMS24 at same DB size/probe count). Client stores m'=2048 hint parities of 256 bits + Hist ~2.5 KB = ~65 KB. Small-domain PRP cost (~139 AES calls per evaluation) not reflected in server/client time.
estimation_meta.reference_db_note: N=2^20 x 32B; structurally similar to RMS24 but with tight ST product. No implementation exists; PRP construction constants unknown.

footnotes: CK20, IshaiShiWichs, Plinko, and WangRen have no implementations. "N=2^20 × 32B" was chosen by the notes author to match Group D conventions and produce comparable asymptotic estimates.

KeywordPIR is a multi-paradigm comparison paper (SealPIR vs. MulPIR vs. Gentry-Ramzan). The 288B config evaluates index PIR; the 40KB config evaluates keyword PIR on a labeled database (100K records).

DistributionalPIR uses real-world datasets (Twitter). Entry sizes vary per user (tweets). The "~560B" for the 38 GB config is the average tweet size. The smaller configs (5K, 10K entries) are synthetic evaluation points.
